sdo:itemDesc.dc:title: Handwritten letter from Brüll to Schenker, dated November 7, 1899
sdo:itemDesc.dc:description: Ignaz Brüll praises Schenker's Syrische Tänze, commenting on its dedication.

tei:opener.tei:salute: Verehrter Herr Doktor!This letter is published in English in Bent, Ian, David Bretherton and William Drabkin, eds, Heinrich Schenker: Selected Correspondence (Woodbridge: Boydell Press, 2014), p. 33.

tei:div.tei:p: Ich erhielt Ihren lieben Brief vom 1. in Abbazia und freute mich mit dessen Inhalt.

Und heute erfreuen Sie mich mit den Syrischen Tänzen u. den sie begleitenden Zeilen.Heinrich Schenker, Syrische Tänze für Pianoforte zu 4 Händen (Vienna: Josef Weinberger, October 1899) bear the inscription: "Alphons frh. v. Rothschild zugeeignet" ("dedicated to Alphons Baron von Rothschild"). OJ 15/12, [4], Weinberger to Schenker, January 5, 1900, implies that the Syrian Dances were to be performed at one of the forthcoming Concerts Gärtner. The concert is announced in the Neue freie Presse, p. 6 (also Neues Wiener Tagblatt, p. 9) for January 6, 1900: “The Eduard Gärtner song evening will take place on Tuesday January 9 in the Bösendorfer Recital Hall. The program includes songs by Mendelssohn, Posa, Brüll, Hugo Wolf, Schütt, Novak and Franz, and in addition a new composition, Syrian Dances for piano four hands by Dr. Heinrich Schenker, will receive its first performance by the composer and Mr. Moriz Violin.” OJ 9/23, [9], Brüll to Schenker, February 14, 1900, confirms that the Dances had by then been reviewed. Ich glaube, daß die Tänze durch ihre eigene melodische Schönheit u. die geniale ‒ auch eigenthümliche ‒ Art Ihrer Bearbeitung Aufsehen erregen werden. Meinen herzlichsten Dank.

Bedarf denn die Widmung einer Erklärung?! Selbst wenn ‒ was ja nicht der Fall ‒ die Widmung neuen Datums wäre, so wüßte doch Jeder, der Sie kennt, daß Ihre Absicht nur sein kann: Ihrem einstigen liebenswürdigen Schüler eine Freude zu bereiten.
I received your nice letter of the 1st in Abbazia and was delighted with its contents.

And today you delight me with your Syrian Dances and the note that accompanied them. I believe that, through their distinctive melodic beauty and the skilful – and original – manner of their development, the Dances will cause a sensation. My most heartfelt thanks.

Does the dedication call for an explanation?! Even if – as is not the case – the dedication were not of recent date, anyone who is acquainted with you knows that your intention can only be to give pleasure to your very dear, erstwhile pupil.
